methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $124M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc

data.narrative:
Quantum Art is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Tal David and Nitzan Akerman that has raised $124M to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 11 peers across public and private markets: 3 public companies (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Stage-progression analysis was the applicable valuation method. Using total capital raised ($124M) as a proxy for current valuation and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage advancement yields an implied valuation of $248M. Peer-median analysis was not executable due to insufficient datapoints within narrow filters—while 7 same-sector peers exist, none met the threshold of three or more comparables when filtered by stage, adjacency, or valuation tier. A sector-wide median would risk material distortion given the heterogeneity of quantum technology approaches and commercialization timelines.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $211M to $285M, centered on $248M. This range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression point estimate. Key uncertainties include the appropriate step-up multiplier for quantum computing specifically (data supported a 2.0x assumption but sector maturity and capital intensity warrant ongoing validation) and the absence of tighter peer comparables to validate whether this quantum-specific company should trade above or below sector medians. Additional data on runway, technical milestones, and customer traction would sharpen this range.
